description: Mock exam passage — the paradox of choice
passage:
It is commonly held that freedom of choice is inherently desirable — that the more options we have, the happier and more fulfilled we become. This assumption underpins much of modern consumer culture, which bombards us with an ever-expanding array of goods, services, and lifestyle possibilities. Yet a growing body of research suggests that this intuition may be fundamentally flawed, and it is worth entertaining the opposite view.

The psychologist Barry Schwartz, in his seminal work on what he calls "the paradox of choice," argues that excessive options do not liberate us; rather, they paralyze us. When confronted with too many alternatives, individuals often experience heightened anxiety, prolonged deliberation, and, paradoxically, diminished satisfaction with whatever they ultimately select. This counterintuitive claim, Schwartz contends, draws on several cognitive and emotional factors that operate below the threshold of conscious awareness.

First, an abundance of choices raises the opportunity cost of any given decision. Every option not taken represents a path foregone — a potential source of regret. As alternatives proliferate, so too does the weight of what we have relinquished. Second, expansive choice sets encourage unrealistic expectations. When the field of possibilities seems boundless, we are inclined to believe that a perfect option must exist, and we feel compelled to find it. Third, decision fatigue sets in. The mental effort required to evaluate dozens of alternatives depletes our cognitive resources, leaving us exhausted and, ironically, more prone to impulsive or regrettable choices.

To mitigate these effects, Schwartz advocates a strategy of deliberate constraint. By voluntarily narrowing the scope of our decisions — whether through personal rules, trusted recommendations, or simply settling for what is "good enough" rather than exhaustively pursuing the optimal — we can reclaim some of the psychological peace that unrestricted choice has inadvertently stripped away. These claims bear serious consideration. The implications extend beyond individual well-being, inviting us to reconsider whether the relentless pursuit of more is genuinely conducive to flourishing, or whether it quietly undermines it.
